🟡 South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group is a voluntary network reimagining ecoregional and bioregional governance for South Asia, challenging rigid nation-state boundaries through biocultural perspectives.

Bioregional Context

South Asia faces multiple intersecting crises—climate chaos, rising nationalism, border conflicts, and ecological degradation. The region's watersheds, like the Ganges, flow through multiple countries, divided by arbitrary borders that fragment ecological and cultural flows.

Place
India
links
https://youtu.be/XFZpg92MIfQ

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 💬 Peer_Learning 🎒 Learning_Programs 🍥 Governance_Design

Background


Across South Asia, climate disruption, nationalism, border conflict, and ecological decline overlap. At the same time, thousands of grassroots initiatives are working for change.

A political map fractures the subcontinent into seven or eight countries—lines drawn by empire and colonial history. The same territory read through watersheds and culture looks different: the Ganges crosses three or four countries, its basin sliced by borders. An upstream state can dam a river with little regard for people, wildlife, or ecology downstream.

Origins and Network


In October 2019, at a Democracy Vikalp Sangam—an Alternatives Confluence in India—participants already gathered to talk about democracy asked why South Asia could not host a bioregional process of its own.

The South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group formed from that question. COVID-19 soon blocked in-person meetings, but work continued online: people kept meeting digitally to reimagine South Asia from biocultural starting points and to question nation-state lines that split ecological flows, cultural exchange, and long-standing economic ties.

The group pursues several aims. One is co-creating knowledge and awareness—advanced partly through reports now feeding modest advocacy. A larger unfinished task is helping communities on the ground reimagine their ecoregions and bioregions and, from there, influencing governance.

Activities

🔸

Learning from Practical Experiments

 🔸

Bioregional Mapping of India

 🔸

Vision for 2100

Vision and Legacy


The South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group represents a growing movement of people across South Asia who believe that reimagining governance from a biocultural perspective isn't just an interesting idea—it's essential for survival in a climate-changed world where rigid political boundaries will only create more conflict and suffering.

They've produced three reports on reimagining South Asia from a bioregional perspective, with another one or two coming out. They've organized webinars learning from bioregional approaches across the world. They're hosting a training course—a certificate course on bioregionalism with about 25 participants, put together by their collaborator ATREE with the bioregionalism working group and some of their other organizations.

They're asking fundamental questions: What if governance matched ecological boundaries? What if cultural flows weren't severed by political lines? What if the people who have lived in these bioregions for centuries could once again move, trade, and care for these landscapes as they did before colonization divided them?

The answers to these questions are still emerging. But in the work of people like Ashish, Nisha, Sonam, and Utham—in their mapping, their research, their visioning, their patient building of relationships across borders—we can see the seeds of a different future taking root.
